summary: Most Indians invest 100% in India. Neil Borate thinks that's a mistake, and the numbers back him up. India is just 4% of global markets. Which means staying India-only isn't diversification. It's a concentrated bet on a small-cap economy while 96% of the world's wealth creation happens elsewhere, in AI, defence, commodities, and sectors we simply don't have on NSE or BSE.

seo.title: Why & How of Global Investing | Neil Borate
seo.description: Most Indians invest 100% in India. Neil Borate thinks that's a mistake, and the numbers back him up. India is just 4% of global markets. Which means

takeaways: India is roughly 4% of global markets, so a 100% India portfolio is a concentrated bet on a small-cap economy, not real diversification.
Whole categories driving global profits, AI, defence, manufacturing and commodities, barely exist on the NSE or BSE, so staying India-only means sitting them out entirely.
There are two practical routes out of India: LRS to open a US broker account step by step, and Gift City, which Neil used to buy DSP's global fund in about 10 minutes from his sofa.
Global investing carries real, knowable costs, remittance, brokerage and taxes, which Neil breaks down rather than glosses over.

This is a portfolio-size question: with a serious India portfolio around 25 to 30 lakh, Neil argues a 30 to 40% global allocation is worth considering, and there are people who should wait or not do it at all.

fork.decision: Should you put some of your money outside India, or keep your whole portfolio at home?

this.label: Allocate globally
points: India is only about 4% of global markets, so India-only is concentration, not diversification.
Sectors driving global profits, AI, defence, manufacturing and commodities, are largely absent from Indian exchanges.
Neil points to the world index versus India across every time horizon as the data point that stumps India bulls.

that.label: Stay India-only for now
points: Global investing adds real costs: remittance, brokerage and taxes that weigh more on a small portfolio.
If your portfolio is still small, the effort may not be worth it yet, and the episode makes the case for waiting.
By Neil's own framing, some investors should not invest globally, so it is not for everyone.

fork.pick: If you already have a serious India portfolio, roughly 25 to 30 lakh or more, start moving toward a 30 to 40% global allocation, and use Gift City if you want the simplest on-ramp. If you are still building your first portfolio, it is reasonable to wait until you cross that size before going global.
